Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, studying about 4 hours most days of the week. I'd usually block out time in the mornings when my mind felt clearest, and then do reading and note-taking in the evenings. With 17 online courses to manage, I had to be organized or I'd fall behind quickly. I kept a written schedule in a notebook since I learn best through reading and writing things down. It wasn't always perfect, but having that structure helped me stay on track despite everything else going on.
Q2: My pattern was fairly regular, but honestly it shifted a lot around deadlines. I'd try to maintain my morning study blocks, but when multiple assignments were due, I'd have to sacrifice sleep or skip other things to keep up. The anxiety of falling behind would push me to study more intensely during those crunch periods. I tried to stay consistent, but life doesn't always cooperate, especially when you're juggling limited resources and trying to perform well.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new assignments posted or to see feedback on work I'd submitted. Sometimes it was just to make sure I hadn't missed any announcements since I couldn't afford to fall behind. The anxiety of potentially missing something important drove a lot of my logins. I'd also go in to access reading materials, though I often downloaded them right away so I wouldn't need to keep logging back in.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking my phone. I'd wake up, make some tea, and log in to see what was new before starting my actual studying. It happened gradually as I realized how many courses I was managing and how easy it was to miss something. The routine helped calm my anxiety a bit because at least I knew I was staying on top of things, even if I felt overwhelmed inside.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were most valuable to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily, making my own summaries. Discussion forums were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The structured assignment guidelines were crucial too because they gave me clear direction when I felt lost or anxious about what was expected.
Q6: I often skipped the video content or watched it at double speed without really absorbing it. Videos just don't work well for how my brain processes information, and they take so much time I don't have. Some of the optional supplementary materials I ignored because I had to prioritize what would actually impact my grades. With limited resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that weren't directly helping me complete assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it helped clarify something I was confused about. I'd ask myself whether this would help me perform better or reduce my stress about upcoming deadlines. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly linked to my grades, it was harder to justify spending time on it. The practical payoff had to be clear, especially given how stretched I already felt.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required work had to come first because my grades depended on it, and I couldn't risk my performance. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff because I knew it might deepen my understanding, but the anxiety about completing required work always won out. It's like optional activities existed in a different category entirely, one I could only access if everything else was perfectly handled, which it never was.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded materials and my own handwritten notes. I'd print readings when I could afford to, or copy important sections into my notebook. I also used some free textbooks I found online and YouTube videos from other educators to supplement what we were given. The LMS was more like a starting point for gathering materials rather than where the real learning happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my notes, rewriting summaries, and working through practice problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, quizzing each other and discussing concepts, which was incredibly helpful but completely invisible online. My best learning often happened away from the screen, just me and my notebooks.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never touch it on the LMS again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet access, which wasn't always reliable for me. This meant my actual engagement with materials was much deeper than my login patterns would suggest. One download could represent hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and engaging felt almost impossible. Midterms were brutal because everything piled up at once across all my courses. I'd feel this heavy sadness settle in, making it hard to focus even when I was sitting with my materials. The CBT sessions helped me push through, but there were days when just opening my notebook felt like climbing a mountain.
Q13: The workload was intense with 17 courses, and fatigue definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted, I'd do the bare minimum online, just checking what was due and downloading what I needed. Confusion about assignments sometimes made me avoid the LMS because logging in and seeing all that work triggered my anxiety. I had to manage my energy carefully, which meant my online presence didn't reflect my actual effort.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through discussion posts or click through modules without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too drained to focus. I'd technically be logged in and active, but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because they triggered my anxiety about saying something wrong in front of peers. Even though I knew participating was important, the fear of judgment made me put it off until the last minute. Sometimes I'd avoid starting big assignments because the scope felt overwhelming, and my brain would freeze up. The avoidance usually made things worse, but in the moment it felt like self-protection.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire relationship with the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached, and my activity would spike dramatically right before submissions. The structure of deadlines helped me prioritize, but it also meant I was often working in a state of high stress. Without deadlines, I'm not sure I would have engaged with certain materials at all, even though that's not ideal.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact on me, sometimes too big. Positive feedback would motivate me to engage more, while critical feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid that course for a day or two. I tried to use feedback constructively to improve my next assignment, but emotionally it was hard not to take criticism personally. The timing of feedback mattered too, getting it quickly helped me stay engaged.
Q18: The course structure helped because it gave me clear milestones and expectations. Having regular deadlines kept me accountable even when my motivation was low. But with 17 courses, different structures sometimes conflicted, and I'd get overwhelmed trying to keep track of everything. I appreciated courses that were straightforward about what was required and when, without too many surprises.
Q19: Courses with clear, written instructions made engagement much easier for me given my learning style. When expectations were ambiguous or buried in video content, I'd struggle and my anxiety would increase. Heavy reliance on synchronous participation was harder because of my schedule and sometimes unreliable internet. The courses that let me work at my own pace with downloadable materials were the ones I engaged with most deeply.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the platform without actually being able to focus on completing work. They might be searching for clarity or reassurance, clicking around trying to understand what's expected. It could also be someone who's struggling with the material and keeps returning to resources without making progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive engagement, sometimes it reflects confusion or worry.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was relatively low because I did most of my studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. But I still performed well because the actual learning was happening, just not in ways the system could track. I think this shows that some students have study methods that don't translate into digital footprints, especially those of us who learn through reading and writing rather than clicking around online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you check in, and what resources you access. But they miss the depth of engagement, whether someone is actually understanding the material or just clicking through. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the quality of learning. For someone like me who downloads everything and studies on paper, the log probably shows a fraction of my actual effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get grades. It's when you're mentally present, asking questions in your head, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement looks like filling notebooks with summaries and annotations, discussing ideas with classmates, and actually thinking about how concepts apply to real situations. It's an internal state that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with my notes spread across my desk, rewriting concepts until they made sense. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I pushed through to complete every assignment, or the sadness that sometimes made studying feel impossible. It can't see the peer study sessions, the moments of genuine understanding, or how much the CBT sessions helped me keep going. My engagement was real and deep, even when my digital presence suggested otherwise.
